name: Remix: Remix: RMS - Restaurant Management System
description:
A professional full-stack Restaurant Management System with order tracking, menu management, and a live dashboard.  1. Project Overview
This is an enterprise-grade Restaurant Management System (RMS) that combines a customer ordering website, a POS system, a real-time kitchen display, and an admin analytics dashboard into a single full-stack application. The system is designed to work like a mini Zomato plus an in-house POS and kitchen management tool, with support for automation and AI-based business insights.

The project started from a basic React + Vite starter template and was gradually expanded into a complete full-stack system with Node.js, Express, MongoDB, real-time sockets, and AI integrations.

2. High-Level Goals
The main goals of this RMS are:

Customers can view the menu, select items, and place orders from a responsive website (or QR-based table ordering).

Orders are sent to the kitchen in real time and tracked via a Kitchen Display System (KDS).

Admins can see detailed analytics such as revenue, total orders, peak hours, top-selling items, and customer behavior.

The system supports automation (notifications, inventory rules, offers) and AI-based insights (sales prediction, recommendations, Instagram captions).

3. Tech Stack and Architecture
The system uses a modern, scalable architecture that can be expanded towards a microservice-style setup:

Backend core: Node.js + Express (modular structure with feature-based modules).

Database:

MongoDB (Mongoose) for core data, logs, analytics, and flexible structures.

PostgreSQL is planned/used for more strict transactional data in the enterprise architecture blueprint.

Frontend: React with Vite for the main app (Customer, Admin, POS, KDS UI).

Real-time: Socket.IO for live order updates between customer, kitchen, and admin.

AI layer: Google Gemini API for business insights and social media content.

Payments: Razorpay integration is designed, but currently optional / disabled until real keys and domain are available.

The codebase is organized with a clear separation between backend (server, models, services) and frontend (components, pages, stores, services). Path aliases (like @/components/…) are configured for cleaner imports.

4. Backend: Features and Logic
The backend is a production-style Node.js + Express server with the following features:

Database & Models

User model: Admins, staff, and customers, with roles for access control.

Menu model: Items with name, price, category, and inventory stock.

Order model: Table, items, total amount, and status (Pending, Preparing, Served, Paid).

Real-time system (Socket.IO)

New orders are pushed to the KDS instantly without page refresh.

Status changes in the kitchen are synchronized in real time to customers and admins.

API endpoints

Menu APIs for loading and managing menu items.

Order APIs for creating orders, checking and updating stock, and handling statuses.

Inventory APIs for updating stock and automatically marking items as “Sold Out”.

Staff APIs for registering and managing staff accounts.

Analytics APIs for calculating revenue, order counts, and trends.

Business logic

Automatic stock deduction when an order is placed; items with zero stock are hidden or marked as sold out.

Optional payment logic: orders can be processed even without Razorpay keys and then manually marked as paid at the counter.

Auto-seeding of initial data: default admin user and sample menu items are created on first run.

Security

Role-based access (admin vs staff).

Mock OTP-based login flow for testing (fixed OTP like 1234) that can later be connected to real SMS gateways.

5. Frontend: Modules and UI
The frontend is a multi-interface React/Vite application with different views for customers, kitchen staff, POS operators, and admins.

5.1 Customer Interface
Home page with hero banner, featured items, categories, and “Order Now” CTA.

Menu page with category filters, search, and item cards (image, name, price, add to cart).

Item detail view with variants, add-ons, and quantity selection.

Cart page with quantity controls, coupon input, and price breakdown (subtotal, tax, discount).

Checkout page with address/notes and payment mode (currently configured for “pay at counter” while Razorpay is optional).

Order tracking page with live status (Pending → Preparing → Ready → Delivered) using sockets.

User profile with order history and quick reorder.

5.2 Kitchen Display System (KDS)
Real-time list of incoming orders as cards showing items, notes, and timers.

Status buttons to move orders through stages (Pending, Preparing, Ready).

Color coding (e.g., green for new, yellow for in-progress, red for delayed).

Delay detection: orders exceeding a time threshold are automatically marked as delayed.

Optional sound alerts and filters (by status, time, priority).

5.3 POS (Staff Interface)
Fast menu selection UI to take walk-in or phone orders.

Table selection and notes entry.

Billing screen with automatic total calculation, tax, and discount support.

Options for printing or sending receipts (design-ready for future integration).

Daily summary view to see total sales and counts per payment mode.

5.4 Admin Dashboard
KPI cards: total revenue, total orders, active/pending orders, new customers.

Charts for sales over time, orders by hour, and peak time heatmaps.

Menu analytics: top-selling and low-performing items.

Customer analytics: repeat vs new customers.

Inventory management: view and update stock, auto “Sold Out” handling.

Staff management: register and manage staff accounts and roles.

6. AI and Automation
The system includes an AI and automation layer to support smarter decisions and marketing:

AI business insights

Uses sales and order data to generate explanations and suggestions, such as demand trends or performance warnings.

AI Instagram caption generator

Admin can generate social media captions for dishes directly from the dashboard.

Automation workflows (designed / partially implemented)

Notify admin when orders are delayed beyond a threshold.

Hide items automatically when stock is low or zero.

Suggest promotions for high-demand items.

These workflows can be extended with Redis queues and cron-style jobs for more complex behavior.

7. Payment Handling
Razorpay integration is prepared on the backend side (creating orders and verifying payments), but since real API keys and domain are not yet available, the current setup treats payment as manual:

Customers place orders, and the system assumes “pay at counter”.

The KDS or admin can mark orders as “Paid” once payment is received offline.

Revenue analytics are calculated based on paid orders.

When real Razorpay keys and a live domain are available, the payment flow can be switched to full online processing without changing the core order logic.

8. Current Status and Fixes Done
According to the action history:

Backend

Core infrastructure, models, APIs, real-time sockets, and basic AI integration are implemented and working.

The system can run even if the cloud MongoDB is not available by falling back to an in-memory database during development.

Frontend

All main components for Customer, KDS, POS, and Admin dashboards are built.

Path alias and folder structure issues (like unresolved imports for @/components/ui/button) were fixed by moving UI components into src/components/ui and aligning Vite/TypeScript config.

Hosting / access

The app has been run in a cloud environment with public URLs for testing.

Localhost access depends on exporting/downloading the ZIP and running it on a local machine with Node.js installed.

9. Development Phases and Roadmap
The work is structured in phases to keep things manageable and scalable:

Phase 1: Backend restructuring, database setup, and basic order flow.

Phase 2: Customer APIs and authentication (OTP-based login).

Phase 3: Customer-facing frontend (menu, cart, checkout, tracking).

Phase 4: Payments and real-time sockets (KDS integration).

Phase 5: Advanced admin dashboards and analytics.

Phase 6: AI features, automation workflows, and further optimization.

The current implementation covers most of these phases at a functional level, with payments left intentionally flexible/optional until real credentials are available.
